summary: Yegge uses Dan Ariely's rejected credit card 'buckets' idea to illustrate a fundamental truth about complex projects: things that seem simple to imagine are monumentally difficult to implement. He traces how an elegant banking idea would balloon into thousands of questions about edge cases, user interfaces, and system integrations. He then applies this lesson to marijuana legalization, demonstrating that what seems like a simple policy change actually requires answering hundreds of specific implementation questions. The core argument is that 'Shit's Easy Syndrome' - the VP belief that anything imaginable is easily executable - ignores the crushing complexity of real-world implementation.
key_points: What VPs call 'simple ideas' become 5,000-page specs when programmers ask the necessary implementation questions
Banks didn't reject the credit bucket idea because of greed - they rejected it because the implementation complexity was astronomical
Laws are essentially programs that must be specified with similar precision, making legal reform a massive software-like project
Every 'simple' policy change like marijuana legalization spawns hundreds of edge cases: advertising rules, prison releases, licensing, interstate commerce, testing standards
Patience is required because complex systems take months, years, or decades to properly implement - overnight change is a pipe dream
money_quotes: YOUR IDEA IS INSANE. It doesn't even matter what it is. It's frigging insane. You are a nut case.
VPs have what my brother Mike refers to as 'Shit's Easy Syndrome.' You know. As in, shit's easy. If it's easy to imagine, then it's easy to implement.
Laws are pretty much like programs. You have to specify their behavior at almost the same level of detail, using a language that's almost as crappy as our programming languages today — English.
Amazon did teach me a valuable lesson, namely that YOUR IDEA IS INSANE.
Shit is NOT easy. Remember that. If you think it's easy, then you are being naïve. You are being a future VP. Don't be that way.
People will die. It's a certainty. Some people are going to die as a direct consequence of legalization of marijuana.
He's not a frigging King, he's a President. He can't make stuff happen overnight by waving his magical sceptre.
key_insight: The gap between 'easy to imagine' and 'possible to implement' is where most ambitious projects go to die, whether in software, banking, or government policy.
